dataset: Cancer iChart - Liverpool Drug Interactions Database

pi_items.source: Web search confirmed Crizotinib is a real cancer drug (ALK/ROS1 inhibitor) approved by FDA in 2011 for treatment of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). Brand name Xalkori. Also confirmed in evidence_docs/red_result_crizotinib_ketoconazole.png showing the app displays 'Crizotinib' in the results.

Web search confirmed Ketoconazole is a real antifungal medication used to treat fungal infections. Also confirmed in evidence_docs/red_result_crizotinib_ketoconazole.png showing the app displays 'Ketoconazole' in the results. Crizotinib-Ketoconazole interaction verified as a documented drug-drug interaction (ketoconazole is a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor increasing crizotinib exposure ~3.2-fold).

Directly observed in evidence_docs/red_result_crizotinib_ketoconazole.png: the Cancer iChart app displays a red banner with the exact text 'Do Not Coadminister' for the Crizotinib + Ketoconazole interaction. Confirmed in evidence_docs/interaction_detail_red.png showing detailed interaction information. Results Key (evidence_docs/results_key.png) confirms red indicators mean 'These drugs should not be coadministered.'

Directly observed in evidence_docs/red_result_crizotinib_ketoconazole.png: the interaction result banner is clearly displayed in red color. Confirmed in evidence_docs/results_key.png which shows the Results Key legend indicating red (filled square) corresponds to 'These drugs should not be coadministered.'

privileged_info_summary: The Cancer iChart app (developed by University of Liverpool and Radboud UMC) displays a red 'Do Not Coadminister' warning banner for the Crizotinib and Ketoconazole drug interaction. This interaction is clinically significant: Crizotinib is a real cancer drug (ALK/ROS1 inhibitor for lung cancer, brand name Xalkori, FDA approved 2011), and Ketoconazole is a real antifungal medication. The interaction occurs because Ketoconazole is a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor that increases crizotinib plasma concentration approximately 3.2-fold, potentially causing dangerous toxicity. The red color in the app's traffic light system indicates the highest level of concern, meaning these drugs should not be coadministered together.
